title: Marzipan fruits
subtitle: Modelled marzipan shaped and painted as miniature fruit
excerpt: Miniature fruits modelled from marzipan, tinted and hand-painted to look like strawberries, lemons, oranges, and apples. A traditional British confectionery skill. Makes about thirty pieces.
sourceNotes: Marzipan modelled to resemble fruit is documented in European confectionery from the medieval period onward, reaching its peak in Victorian and Edwardian British confectionery shops. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: marzipan fruits are recorded in the confectionery chapter as a domestic sweetmeat made from almond paste tinted and shaped to resemble fresh fruit. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: coloured and modelled marzipan is documented as a Victorian home confectionery skill with notes on tinting with vegetable-based food colours.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a tin or box at room temperature for up to 1 month. Leave uncovered for the first hour to develop a dry skin.

content.text: Marzipan fruit is an exercise in working in very small quantities. Pinch off a piece of about 15 g per fruit, knead in the colour until even, and shape by hand. The icing sugar on the work surface prevents sticking. Cloves make convincing apple and pear stalks; a toothpick dragged lightly over a strawberry shape creates the dimpled texture.

items.prepNote: gel colours in red, yellow, orange, green

content.text: Dust the work surface lightly with {{icing-sugar}}. Divide the {{marzipan}} into four equal portions of 125 g. Knead a tiny amount of {{food-colouring}} (red, orange, yellow, or green) into each portion, working the colour in thoroughly until even. A cocktail stick dipped into gel colour picks up the right amount for a small portion; add more by degrees rather than all at once.

Pinch off pieces of about 15 g each and shape by hand: strawberries are a rounded cone with a dimpled surface made by rolling over a fine grater or prodding with a toothpick; lemons are an oval with pointed ends; oranges are a ball with dimpled skin made by rolling over a rough surface; apples and pears are recognisable teardrop shapes with a {{cloves}} stem pressed into the top. Leave on a tray lined with baking paper for at least 1 hour to form a dry skin before boxing.
